name: Sammy Price
abstract: Samuel Blythe Price (October 6, 1908 – April 14, 1992) was an American jazz, boogie-woogie and jump blues pianist and bandleader. Price's playing is dark, mellow, and relaxed rather than percussive, and he was a specialist at creating the appropriate mood and swing for blues and rhythm and blues recordings.

quotes.text: But the problem that they were having, they didn't have enough money to write the score and in some way I was involved in helping to get that score prepared, and helping them to present "Yamacraw" in Carnegie Hall. But the tragedy of the thing was that the day that we were having the concert, was the date that Roosevelt died, and the date that Roosevelt died, the world stood still. So it meant that we had to have an adjournment; we had to postpone the thing for a week and the enthusiasm wasn't there as it was for the first thing. As a result, I don't know...I don't think I made a profit. I didn't care. I didn't want to make a profit 'cause, I told you, I
quotes.caption: Sammy Price recalls postponing 'Yamacraw' concert due to FDR's death

quotes.text: some money you'd say, 'Now, when you get down in Shreveport, give this to my aunt and stay with her that week.' And maybe coming from the other way, Louisville, you come back down to Winston-Salem and back down and all until you get to Shreveport and you give the money to the parent or the mother of the friend, and so forth. So, I think that, that one thing that that thing taught you was fidelity, friendship and respect and recognition. Now we don't practice that all the time, that's an important fact. For example, I may pan some guy, you know, that's human nature to talk about a guy behind his back, though when you're with him...you know what I mean...when you're with him you don't really mean it, you're just running your mouth. And of all the piano players that I've played with, I don't really say that a guy can't play. I may not like it, but I've come into some great music... great piano players, you know...Donald Lambert, you know, James P. Johnson. One year when I was successful in New York and was working for Decca I had an office at 1674 Broadway. You know that I gave Willie the Lion Smith and James P. Johnson an office with a piano where they could come every day and, you know, and practice, and I did it just because...out of respect for them. And then I helped to promote James P.'s 'Yamacraw' at Carnegie Hall. And I always
quotes.caption: Mentioning helping promote James P. Johnson at Carnegie Hall

quotes.text: He managed Adam's political affairs and he was also District Leader and he was in the Borough President's office, and he was a kingmaker, of course. He never sought any political office, but people running for office always tried to get his support in order to, you know, function and to be successful. And he helped to elect Roosevelt.../this was about the time/ well, the last time we re-elected Roosevelt and then he died. And Roosevelt died at the time that my uncle had helped me get this booking agency license. And I was on Broadway still working for Decca and I had been approached by James P. Johnson's people to help with the "Yamacraw" concert that they were having in Carnegie Hall.
quotes.caption: Mention of James P. Johnson's Yamacraw concert

quotes.text: But as I sit here now, I'd just like to see if that man that charged me twenty-five cents a lesson...I'd like to see his eyes open if they told him that I played at BBC in London and CBS in New York and NBC, and Swiss television and French television and the Olympia in Paris and the Salle Gevaux and the Salle Pleyel and the Fair de Paris and concerts in Gobr and Amsterdam, and Carnegie Hall in New York and the Academy of Music in Philadelphia and Town Hall in New York. I'd just like to see what the expression on his face would be like, and what he would say. I imagine it would be something like, 'Well, sometimes kids have hidden talents. (Laughter). Sometimes it's latent and it don't come out... it don't come out (you know )soon.'
quotes.caption: Sammy Price lists Carnegie Hall among his performance venues

about_quotes.evidence: He recorded under that name, and apparently on December 29, 1955, he recorded with you... How long were you with Sam Price? ... Just that tour, yes.

about_quotes.evidence: I was working at the Jazz Limited in Chicago... I went over with Sammy Price. We made the same, about the same tour -- in Paris, France.

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Coleman
about_quotes.evidence: One Newspaper said that the personnels read like a 'Who's Who' in jazz. The session was played by forming three orchestras with those musicians... Art Hodes, Dick Carey, Ellis Larkins and Sammy Price, pianists.

about_quotes.speaker: Danny Barker
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] Johnny Temple... I made quite a few of those things with Sam Price... [INTERVIEWEE] He'd bring them in, yeah. That was Mel Williams' right-hand man.

about_quotes.speaker: Herman Autrey
about_quotes.evidence: I guess it must have been in the forties, you were making some records for Decca, and for Vocalion, with Mabel Robinson, Sam Price, with Gene Sedric.
